title: Pricing in the New Year: Why Inflation Behaves Differently at the Start of the Year
summary: Boston Fed researchers analyze three factors driving elevated January inflation: residual seasonality in CPI data, higher frequency of price changes at the start of the year (over 28% of prices change from December to January), and compositional effects where sectors with the strongest tendency to change prices in January experienced particularly high inflation from 2023-2025. The research uses BLS price microdata and covers the 1998-2025 period.
key_points: January average seasonally adjusted core CPI inflation is 0.23%, 0.03 percentage points above the all-month average of 0.20%, indicating residual seasonality in CPI adjustment
Over 28% of prices change from December to January on average, the highest frequency of any month in the year
During periods of elevated inflation (2023-2025), more frequent price changes amplify January inflation because prices rise more when they change
The frequency of price increases is greatest in January and declines substantially through the year, while price decreases are lower at the start of the year
Sectors with the highest frequency of January price changes (e.g., medical goods and services) have experienced relatively higher inflation in 2023-2025
The correlation between January price-change frequency and inflation was stronger in 2023-2025 than in the 1998-2022 period
The authors note findings are solely their own and not official Federal Reserve Bank of Boston views
why_it_matters: This research is relevant for markets and policymakers interpreting January CPI data, which is often treated as a signal for underlying inflation trends. Understanding that January inflation is mechanically elevated due to pricing patterns—independent of underlying demand or supply conditions—can prevent misreading seasonal data as genuine changes in inflation dynamics. This matters for Fed policy communications and market pricing of rate expectations.
macro_context: The research examines structural reasons why January CPI prints tend to run higher than other months. During elevated inflation periods (2023-2025), this January effect was amplified because higher base inflation meant each price change resulted in larger increases. The findings suggest that January 2026 CPI data, released February 11, should be interpreted with awareness of these seasonal and compositional factors rather than treated as evidence of accelerating or decelerating underlying trends.
important_numbers: 0.23% average January seasonally adjusted core CPI inflation rate
0.20% average all-month seasonally adjusted core CPI inflation rate
0.03 percentage point residual seasonality effect in January
28%+ of prices change from December to January

issues_found: source_type in record correctly reflects 'data_release' per metadata, but metadata PAGE_TYPE is incorrectly labeled. The actual source is a research paper/analysis piece, not a data release. However, this metadata discrepancy does not affect the record's accuracy since event_type correctly uses 'research_paper'.

human_review.notes: Source body was truncated at end of Figure 4 analysis. The regression results for the 1998-2022 period were mentioned but cut off. This may be a minor gap but the core three-reason framework and key quantitative findings are captured.
